reasoning: The base rate for fatalities in the British Virgin Islands (BVI) is extremely low. The ACLED dataset indicates a baseline reference value of 1.000 as of December 11, 2025, which corresponds to exactly zero conflict-related fatalities in the preceding 360 days. To resolve 'YES', the BVI would need to experience more than 10 fatalities (i.e., at least 11) in the 30 days leading up to each resolution date. Given the BVI's small population (~35,000) and very low historical homicide rate (typically 1-2 per year), seeing 11 or more ACLED-qualifying fatalities in a single 30-day period would require an unprecedented catastrophic event, such as a mass shooting or severe civil breakdown, which there are absolutely no precursors for at this time. Current news from Nov/Dec 2025 shows no instances of major violence. I set the probability to the minimum allowed bound (0.05) for the near-term resolution date, as the remaining days are highly unlikely to accumulate 11 deaths, and incrementally increase it to 0.07 for the furthest date to account for increasing uncertainty over time.
